past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 15 years ago, variably compliant with medications
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 9 years ago
Obstructive sleep apnea, prescribed CPAP but uses it intermittently
Chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL)
family_history: Father had a stroke at age 71 with residual aphasia
Mother with hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died at age 79 of heart failure
Sister with atrial fibrillation diagnosed at age 62

social_history.smoking: Active smoker, approximately 20 pack-year history, currently half a pack per day
social_history.occupation: Long-haul truck driver, currently on medical leave due to poorly controlled blood pressure
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a ground-floor apartment; divorced, two adult children in the area
social_history.exercise: Minimal regular exercise, occasional walks around the neighborhood
social_history.diet: Admits to a high-salt, high-fat diet with frequent fast food

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but mildly drowsy. Oriented to person and place but initially states the wrong month (says February instead of the current month). Speech is mildly dysarthric with occasional word-finding pauses. Can follow two-step commands. Names 5/6 common objects. Mild inattention noted on serial sevens (makes two errors). Right-sided neglect suspected: fails to identify stimuli in the right visual field on double simultaneous stimulation, though isolated right visual field testing appears grossly intact.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal at 3 mm, reactive. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No clear gaze preference, though tends to look leftward when unengaged. Mild right lower facial flattening compared to left, more apparent with spontaneous smile than with voluntary effort. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 4/5 strength at deltoid, biceps, and grip with subtle pronator drift on sustained arm extension over 10 seconds. Right lower extremity: 4+/5 hip flexion, 5-/5 at ankle dorsiflexion. Left upper and lower extremities: 5/5 strength throughout. No tremor or involuntary movements. Normal bulk bilaterally.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased pinprick sensation over the right hand and forearm compared to left. Right-sided extinction on double simultaneous stimulation in both upper extremities. Light touch and proprioception appear grossly intact bilaterally on isolated testing.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Right biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+, patellar 3+, Achilles 2+. Left side: all 2+ and symmetric. Plantar response equivocal on the right (neither clearly upgoing nor clearly flexor), flexor on the left.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose mildly imprecise on the right with subtle overshoot on terminal approach; intact on the left. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly unsteady, tends to drift rightward. Narrow-based. No steppage or circumduction. Able to walk independently but cautiously.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 6. Subtle right-sided hemineglect present on line bisection (bisects 2 cm to the left of center). No meningismus. No carotid bruits appreciated.

patient.chief_complaint: Woke up with slurred speech and clumsiness of the right hand
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Reginald Carter is a 67-year-old right-handed African American man with a history of poorly controlled hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, obstructive sleep apnea, and chronic kidney disease who presents to the emergency department after waking at approximately 06:45 this morning with new-onset slurred speech and difficulty using his right hand. He reports going to bed at approximately 23:00 the previous evening feeling entirely at his baseline. He woke once around 03:00 to use the bathroom and does not recall any difficulty at that time, though he acknowledges he was groggy and did not pay close attention to his coordination or speech. When his alarm went off at 06:45, he noticed his speech sounded thick and he had trouble gripping his phone. He also felt mildly unsteady when walking to the bathroom. He initially attributed these symptoms to having slept in an awkward position and waited approximately 45 minutes to see if they would resolve. When the symptoms persisted, his daughter called him at 07:30 during her morning routine, noticed his slurred speech over the phone, and urged him to call 911. EMS arrived at 07:52 and identified right facial droop and mild right arm weakness. A prehospital stroke alert was activated with a Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale score of 2. His fingerstick glucose in the field was 204 mg/dL. He arrived at the ED at 08:14. On initial assessment, the patient reports a mild headache over the left temporal region but denies any visual changes, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, or seizure-like activity. He cannot say for certain whether the symptoms were present when he woke at 03:00, making the last known well time approximately 23:00 the previous evening, roughly 9 hours prior to ED arrival. This exceeds the standard 4.5-hour window for IV thrombolysis and complicates acute management decisions. He has no history of prior stroke or TIA. He ran out of his amlodipine approximately one week ago and has not refilled it.

findings.location: Left posterior frontal cortex and left parietal lobe, predominantly involving the left MCA superior division territory
findings.size: Approximately 2.8 x 1.9 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true acute diffusion restriction
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense signal in the affected region, suggesting onset at least several hours prior
signal_characteristics.T1: Subtle hypointensity in the left posterior frontal and parietal cortex
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal corresponding to the DWI abnormality

findings.mass_effect: No significant mass effect. No midline shift.
findings.borders: Margins follow a cortical and subcortical distribution consistent with left MCA superior division territory

findings.location: Bilateral periventricular and deep white matter

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Multifocal bilateral periventricular and subcortical white matter hyperintensities, Fazekas grade 2-3

additional_observations: Moderate burden of chronic white matter ischemic changes complicating identification of the acute lesion on FLAIR; DWI is critical for delineation
MRA of the circle of Willis demonstrates mild irregularity of the left MCA M2 superior division branch without complete occlusion; no M1 occlusion identified
Bilateral ACA, right MCA, and posterior circulation vessels are patent
Mild generalized cerebral volume loss, slightly more than expected for age
mri.impression: 1. Acute left MCA superior division territory infarction involving the posterior frontal and parietal cortex, as confirmed by restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC. FLAIR positivity suggests onset greater than 4-6 hours. 2. Moderate-to-severe chronic small vessel ischemic disease (Fazekas 2-3), partially obscuring the acute lesion on FLAIR sequences. 3. Mild irregularity of the left MCA M2 superior division without frank occlusion on MRA; no large vessel occlusion. 4. No hemorrhagic transformation.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute ischemic infarction, left MCA superior division territory
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction in a vascular territory with corresponding FLAIR changes; clinical correlation with right-sided deficits and neglect
Distribution follows a vascular territory rather than periventricular or juxtacortical pattern; no incomplete ring enhancement expected
No mass effect, no ring enhancement, and DWI pattern consistent with cytotoxic rather than vasogenic edema

recommended_actions: CTA head and neck to further characterize vascular anatomy and potential embolic source
Consider CT perfusion if extended window reperfusion therapy is being contemplated
Follow-up MRI in 24-48 hours to assess for hemorrhagic transformation
Cardiac workup including ECG and echocardiography to evaluate for cardioembolic source

findings: Left atrial enlargement suggested by notched P waves in lead II (P-wave duration >120 ms) and deep negative terminal deflection in V1
Left ventricular hypertrophy by Sokolow-Lyon criteria (SV1 + RV5 = 38 mm)
Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6)
No acute ST elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm with left atrial enlargement and left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. Nonspecific lateral ST-T wave changes, likely strain pattern related to LVH. No atrial fibrillation detected on this tracing. No acute ischemic changes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Left atrial enlargement raises the possibility of underlying paroxysmal atrial fibrillation as a potential cardioembolic source, even though sinus rhythm is present on this tracing. LVH is consistent with chronic hypertension. Prolonged cardiac rhythm monitoring is recommended to evaluate for occult arrhythmia.

CBC.clinical_significance: Mildly low, may reflect chronic disease or nutritional factors

BMP.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with known CKD stage 3a and possible mild dehydration
Mildly elevated, near patient's known baseline of 1.4 mg/dL; consistent with CKD stage 3a
Elevated, consistent with poorly controlled diabetes and acute stress hyperglycemia

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Elevated despite statin therapy; suboptimal control
Elevated, above goal for high-risk patient; statin dose intensification warranted
Elevated, consistent with metabolic syndrome and poor dietary habits

Cardiac and Other.clinical_significance: Poorly controlled diabetes, significant cerebrovascular risk factor
Consistent with CKD stage 3a; relevant for medication dosing decisions

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results demonstrate poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%, glucose 218 mg/dL), chronic kidney disease stage 3a (creatinine 1.5, eGFR 48), suboptimal lipid control despite statin therapy, and mild anemia. Coagulation studies are normal. Troponin is within normal range. These findings reflect multiple inadequately managed vascular risk factors contributing to cerebrovascular disease burden.

findings.location: Left MCA M2 superior division branch

signal_characteristics.CTA: Mild focal narrowing of the left MCA M2 superior division with residual flow distally. No complete occlusion. Possible partial recanalization of prior embolic occlusion or focal atherosclerotic stenosis.

findings.location: Bilateral carotid bifurcations and left ICA origin

signal_characteristics.CTA: Mixed calcified and soft plaque at the left ICA origin with approximately 55-60% stenosis (NASCET criteria). Right ICA with approximately 30% stenosis. Bilateral vertebral artery origins patent.

additional_observations: No large vessel occlusion identified in the anterior or posterior circulation
Aortic arch is type II with mild atheromatous disease
Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with normal caliber
Mild intracranial atherosclerotic disease of the bilateral MCAs
output.impression: 1. Mild focal narrowing of the left MCA M2 superior division, likely representing partial recanalization of a prior embolic occlusion or focal intracranial atherosclerosis. No large vessel occlusion amenable to thrombectomy. 2. Moderate left ICA stenosis (55-60%, NASCET criteria) with mixed plaque; consideration for medical optimization versus carotid intervention depending on clinical course. 3. Mild bilateral intracranial atherosclerotic disease.
recommended_actions: Medical optimization of atherosclerotic risk factors including high-intensity statin and antihypertensives
Consider carotid duplex ultrasound for serial monitoring of left ICA stenosis
Evaluate for cardioembolic source given absence of high-grade carotid stenosis or large vessel occlusion
Prolonged cardiac monitoring to rule out paroxysmal atrial fibrillation

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mildly dilated left atrium; raises suspicion for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Consistent with chronic poorly controlled hypertension
Common in hypertensive heart disease, associated with elevated filling pressures
Degenerative change; no hemodynamic significance currently

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: No PFO or ASD identified with agitated saline contrast

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates preserved left ventricular systolic function with concentric LVH and grade I diastolic dysfunction consistent with longstanding hypertension. Left atrium is mildly dilated (LAVI 36 mL/m2), raising suspicion for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation as a possible cardioembolic source. No intracardiac thrombus or PFO identified. Bubble study negative. Overall findings are consistent with hypertensive heart disease.

output.rhythm: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm with intermittent atrial fibrillation
findings: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm for approximately 20.5 hours of the 24-hour recording
Three episodes of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation totaling 3.5 hours (14.6% AF burden)
Longest AF episode: 1 hour 52 minutes, occurring between 02:14 and 04:06
Average ventricular rate during AF episodes: 108 bpm (range 88-132 bpm)
Frequent premature atrial complexes: 847 in 24 hours (3.5% of total beats)
No significant pauses (longest RR interval 1.8 seconds)
output.interpretation: Twenty-four hour Holter monitoring reveals paroxysmal atrial fibrillation with a 14.6% AF burden across three discrete episodes. The longest episode occurred during the early morning hours (02:14-04:06), which is temporally relevant to the patient's wake-up stroke presentation. Frequent PACs during sinus rhythm suggest underlying atrial substrate disease.
output.clinical_correlation: The detection of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, particularly the episode occurring during the early morning hours, provides a likely cardioembolic mechanism for this patient's ischemic stroke. This finding has critical implications for secondary prevention: anticoagulation therapy is indicated rather than antiplatelet therapy alone. CHA2DS2-VASc score for this patient is 5 (age 65-74, hypertension, diabetes, stroke/TIA, vascular disease), conferring a high annual stroke risk without anticoagulation.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated; associated with increased vascular risk. May be related to CKD or dietary factors. Consider folate and B12 supplementation.

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel is unremarkable for hereditary or acquired thrombophilia. Mildly elevated homocysteine (16.8 umol/L) noted, which can be associated with increased vascular risk and may be related to the patient's CKD and dietary habits. No antiphospholipid syndrome identified. The stroke mechanism is most likely cardioembolic (paroxysmal AF) or atherothrombotic given the extensive vascular risk factor profile.

findings.location: Left internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

signal_characteristics.Plaque_morphology: Heterogeneous plaque with echolucent components suggesting vulnerable plaque

findings.location: Right internal carotid artery at the bifurcation

additional_observations: Common carotid arteries demonstrate normal flow bilaterally
Bilateral vertebral arteries are patent with antegrade flow
Bilateral external carotid arteries are patent
output.impression: 1. Moderate left ICA stenosis (50-69% NASCET criteria) with heterogeneous, potentially vulnerable plaque. 2. Mild right ICA stenosis (<50%) with calcified, stable-appearing plaque. 3. Patent vertebral arteries bilaterally. The left ICA stenosis with vulnerable plaque morphology may represent an additional or alternative embolic source to the left MCA territory infarct, particularly if paroxysmal AF is not confirmed.
recommended_actions: Intensive medical management of atherosclerotic risk factors
High-intensity statin therapy for plaque stabilization
Follow-up carotid duplex in 6 months to monitor for progression
If stenosis progresses to 70% or greater, consider carotid endarterectomy or stenting in the context of the symptomatic territory

findings.location: Left posterior frontal and parietal cortex
findings.size: Core infarct volume approximately 18 mL; minimal mismatch tissue approximately 8 mL; mismatch ratio 1.4

signal_characteristics.CBF: Reduced in left posterior frontal and parietal cortex
signal_characteristics.CBV: Reduced in the same region, suggesting completed infarction
signal_characteristics.MTT: Mildly prolonged in a slightly larger territory than the CBV abnormality

additional_observations: Mismatch ratio of 1.4 does not meet the 1.8 threshold for late window thrombectomy per DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria
No large vessel occlusion to target with mechanical intervention
output.impression: CT perfusion demonstrates a small completed infarct in the left posterior frontal and parietal region (core 18 mL) with minimal salvageable penumbral tissue (8 mL, mismatch ratio 1.4). The absence of a significant target mismatch profile and the lack of a large vessel occlusion indicate that late-window endovascular intervention is not indicated. Medical management is recommended.
recommended_actions: Medical management of ischemic stroke is the appropriate treatment pathway
Focus on identifying and treating the underlying stroke mechanism for secondary prevention
Serial neurological examinations to monitor for clinical deterioration

output.query: wake-up stroke thrombolysis MRI DWI-FLAIR mismatch management guidelines

results.title: MRI-Guided Thrombolysis for Stroke with Unknown Time of Onset (WAKE-UP Trial)
results.authors: Thomalla G, Simonsen CZ, et al.
results.key_finding: IV alteplase improved functional outcome in wake-up stroke patients selected by DWI-FLAIR mismatch (DWI positive, FLAIR negative), indicating onset within ~4.5 hours.

results.title: Extending thrombolysis to 4.5-9 hours using perfusion imaging (EXTEND Trial)
results.authors: Ma H, Campbell BCV, et al.
results.key_finding: IV alteplase between 4.5-9h from onset or in wake-up stroke benefited patients with penumbral mismatch on CT or MRI perfusion imaging.

results.title: 2019 AHA/ASA Guideline Update for Early Management of Acute Ischemic Stroke
results.authors: Powers WJ, Rabinstein AA, et al.
results.key_finding: In wake-up stroke, if DWI-FLAIR mismatch is present, IV alteplase may be considered. If FLAIR is already positive, this suggests onset >4.5 hours and standard thrombolysis criteria may not be met.

output.summary: For wake-up strokes with unknown onset time, the WAKE-UP trial demonstrated that DWI-FLAIR mismatch on MRI can identify patients who may benefit from IV thrombolysis. A DWI-positive/FLAIR-negative mismatch suggests the stroke occurred within approximately 4.5 hours and the patient may be treated with alteplase. However, when both DWI and FLAIR are positive (as in this case), the stroke likely occurred more than 4.5 hours ago, and standard IV thrombolysis is generally not recommended. The EXTEND trial showed that CT or MRI perfusion-based selection can extend the treatment window to 9 hours, but requires a significant penumbral mismatch. Without a target mismatch profile or large vessel occlusion, medical management with focus on secondary prevention is the appropriate strategy.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left MCA superior division territory, likely cardioembolic etiology from paroxysmal atrial fibrillation

differential.diagnosis: Large artery atherosclerotic stroke (artery-to-artery embolism from left ICA plaque)
differential.key_distinguishing: Moderate left ICA stenosis with vulnerable plaque is present, but the detection of paroxysmal AF on Holter monitoring favors cardioembolic mechanism; both mechanisms may be contributory
MRI shows DWI restriction without hemorrhage; CT/MRI definitively excludes hemorrhagic etiology
No seizure activity reported; deficits have persisted for hours; MRI confirms acute infarction on DWI
Patient age 67 with no migraine history; acute DWI-positive lesion on MRI rules out migraine mimic
Field glucose 204 mg/dL and ED glucose 218 mg/dL; hypoglycemia excluded as cause of focal deficits

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code and perform rapid clinical assessment including NIHSS, establish last known well time, and obtain fingerstick glucose
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 6, last known well approximately 9 hours prior (23:00 previous evening), glucose 204 mg/dL

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No hemorrhage; possible subtle hypodensity in left frontal-parietal region

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, troponin, lipid panel, and HbA1c
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal coagulation, hyperglycemia, elevated creatinine (CKD), poorly controlled diabetes, dyslipidemia

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate cardiac rhythm
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sinus rhythm with left atrial enlargement and LVH; no AF on this tracing but LAE raises suspicion

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with DWI to evaluate for acute infarction and assess DWI-FLAIR mismatch for potential thrombolysis eligibility
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute DWI restriction in left MCA superior division territory; FLAIR positive indicating onset >4.5 hours; no DWI-FLAIR mismatch

optimal_actions.action: Determine that IV thrombolysis is NOT indicated: last known well >4.5 hours, no DWI-FLAIR mismatch on MRI, FLAIR already positive
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient does not meet criteria for IV alteplase per WAKE-UP trial or standard guidelines

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck to evaluate for large vessel occlusion and vascular anatomy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No large vessel occlusion; moderate left ICA stenosis (55-60%); mild left MCA M2 irregularity

optimal_actions.action: Initiate dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) for early secondary prevention pending stroke mechanism workup
Order prolonged cardiac monitoring (Holter or telemetry) to evaluate for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation given left atrial enlargement on ECG and echo
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation detected with 14.6% AF burden

optimal_actions.action: Order transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate cardiac structure and function and assess for cardioembolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: LVH, dilated left atrium, diastolic dysfunction, no thrombus or PFO

optimal_actions.action: Once paroxysmal AF is confirmed, transition from antiplatelet to anticoagulation therapy with a DOAC (e.g., apixaban 5 mg BID, with dose adjustment consideration for renal function)

Optimize vascular risk factor management: restart amlodipine, intensify statin to high-dose (rosuvastatin 40 mg or atorvastatin 80 mg), optimize diabetes management, counsel on smoking cessation and CPAP adherence
Admit to stroke unit for monitoring, serial neurological assessments, and rehabilitation evaluation

critical_actions: Establish last known well time to determine thrombolysis eligibility; recognize that wake-up stroke with FLAIR-positive MRI excludes standard IV thrombolysis
Obtain emergent CT head to exclude hemorrhage
Obtain brain MRI with DWI to confirm acute infarction and assess DWI-FLAIR mismatch
Obtain ECG and recognize left atrial enlargement as a clue to possible paroxysmal AF
Order prolonged cardiac monitoring to detect paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
Initiate anticoagulation rather than antiplatelet therapy once paroxysmal AF is confirmed
Do NOT administer IV alteplase given the FLAIR-positive wake-up stroke beyond the standard treatment window
contraindicated_actions: Administering IV alteplase when last known well time exceeds 4.5 hours and DWI-FLAIR mismatch is absent (FLAIR already positive)
Pursuing mechanical thrombectomy in the absence of a large vessel occlusion or target mismatch profile
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the first 24-48 hours (risk of worsening ischemia in the absence of thrombolysis)
Relying solely on a single ECG tracing to exclude atrial fibrillation as the stroke mechanism
Continuing antiplatelet-only therapy after paroxysmal AF is documented; anticoagulation is mandatory for secondary prevention
Administering contrast-enhanced imaging without awareness of CKD stage 3a and need for renal-protective measures
key_reasoning_points: Wake-up stroke with unknown onset time requires DWI-FLAIR mismatch assessment on MRI to determine thrombolysis eligibility; FLAIR positivity in this case indicates onset >4.5 hours ago, precluding standard IV tPA
Normal sinus rhythm on initial ECG does not exclude paroxysmal atrial fibrillation; left atrial enlargement on ECG and echocardiogram should prompt prolonged cardiac monitoring
The detection of paroxysmal AF fundamentally changes secondary prevention strategy from antiplatelet to anticoagulation therapy

Moderate left ICA stenosis with vulnerable plaque represents a competing stroke mechanism; both artery-to-artery embolism and cardioembolism may contribute, but AF detection takes therapeutic priority
Chronic small vessel ischemic disease on MRI (Fazekas 2-3) can obscure acute findings on FLAIR; DWI sequences are essential for identifying the acute lesion

The right-sided neglect with a left hemisphere lesion is the key lateralizing finding; subtle neglect in a dominant hemisphere stroke is an atypical feature that can be missed without careful examination

CKD stage 3a (eGFR 48) impacts contrast administration decisions and anticoagulant dosing; apixaban does not require dose reduction until eGFR <25 or if two of three criteria are met (age >=80, weight <=60 kg, creatinine >=1.5)

Multiple poorly controlled risk factors (hypertension off medication, diabetes with HbA1c 8.4%, active smoking, dyslipidemia, untreated OSA) all contributed to stroke risk and must be addressed comprehensively

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate - wake-up stroke with subtle right-sided neglect and mild dysarthria, no AF on initial ECG but left atrial enlargement as a clue, chronic white matter disease complicating MRI interpretation
clinical_pearls: The WAKE-UP trial established DWI-FLAIR mismatch as a tissue clock for wake-up strokes: DWI+/FLAIR- suggests onset within 4.5 hours and eligibility for thrombolysis; DWI+/FLAIR+ suggests onset beyond 4.5 hours
Up to 25% of cryptogenic strokes in patients over 55 are attributed to paroxysmal AF detected on prolonged monitoring; a single ECG is insufficient to exclude this diagnosis
Left atrial enlargement on ECG or echocardiography is a strong predictor of underlying paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and should lower the threshold for prolonged cardiac monitoring
Hemineglect with a dominant (left) hemisphere lesion is less common and typically more subtle than with non-dominant (right) hemisphere strokes, making it easier to miss on routine examination
